title: Argo CD Multi-Environment Repository Structure Checklist
description: How to organize your Git repositories when running Argo CD across dev, staging, and production. Covers folder layout, app-of-apps, ApplicationSets, secrets, RBAC, and promotion flow.

items.title: Split application code from deployment manifests
items.description: Keep your app source code in one repo and your Kubernetes manifests in a separate config repo. Mixing them means every code commit triggers a sync and your Argo CD diff history becomes noisy. A dedicated config repo also lets platform engineers control deployments without touching application code.

codeBlocks.label: Two-repo layout
codeBlocks.code:
# App repo (built by CI, produces container images)
org/api-service
├── src/
├── Dockerfile
├── .github/workflows/build.yaml   # builds image, opens PR to config repo
└── README.md

# Config repo (consumed by Argo CD)
org/k8s-config
├── apps/
├── infrastructure/
├── clusters/
└── README.md

# Why two repos:
# - revert a bad deploy without reverting code
# - different review rules (config repo needs platform approval)
# - smaller blast radius for credentials

items.title: Pick a base + overlay pattern (Kustomize or Helm)
items.description: Do not copy-paste manifests across environments. Three months in you will have drift between dev and prod that nobody can explain. Use Kustomize overlays or Helm values files so each environment is a thin diff over a shared base.

codeBlocks.label: Kustomize base + overlays layout
codeBlocks.code:
k8s-config/
└── apps/
    └── api-service/
        ├── base/
        │   ├── deployment.yaml
        │   ├── service.yaml
        │   ├── hpa.yaml
        │   └── kustomization.yaml
        └── overlays/
            ├── dev/
            │   ├── kustomization.yaml
            │   └── replicas-patch.yaml
            ├── staging/
            │   ├── kustomization.yaml
            │   └── replicas-patch.yaml
            └── prod/
                ├── kustomization.yaml
                ├── replicas-patch.yaml
                └── pdb.yaml

codeBlocks.label: Production overlay
codeBlocks.code:
# apps/api-service/overlays/prod/kustomization.yaml
apiVersion: kustomize.config.k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Kustomization

namespace: api-prod

resources:
  - ../../base
  - pdb.yaml

images:
  - name: api-service
    newTag: v1.4.2  # pinned, never :latest

patches:
  - path: replicas-patch.yaml
    target:
      kind: Deployment
      name: api-service

commonLabels:
  environment: production
  team: platform

items.title: Use the app-of-apps pattern as your entry point
items.description: Bootstrap each cluster with a single root Application that points to a folder of child Applications. Without this you will be running kubectl apply by hand every time you onboard a new service. The root app gives you one place to add or remove workloads per environment.

codeBlocks.label: Root app for the prod cluster
codeBlocks.code:
# clusters/prod/root-app.yaml
apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1
kind: Application
metadata:
  name: root-prod
  namespace: argocd
spec:
  project: default
  source:
    repoURL: https://github.com/org/k8s-config
    targetRevision: main
    path: clusters/prod/apps   # folder full of Application manifests
  destination:
    server: https://kubernetes.default.svc
    namespace: argocd
  syncPolicy:
    automated:
      prune: true
      selfHeal: true

codeBlocks.label: Bootstrap the cluster
codeBlocks.code:
# Apply the root app once per cluster
kubectl apply -f clusters/prod/root-app.yaml

# Argo CD will now create every child Application in clusters/prod/apps/
# From here on you add a new YAML file to that folder and Argo CD picks it up

ls clusters/prod/apps/
# api-service.yaml
# web-frontend.yaml
# worker.yaml
# cron-jobs.yaml

items.title: Use ApplicationSet to fan out across environments
items.description: Once you have more than two environments, hand-writing one Application per env per service gets old fast. ApplicationSet generators read a list of clusters or directories and produce Applications automatically. New environment? Add one entry, get every app deployed.

codeBlocks.label: Cluster generator ApplicationSet
codeBlocks.code:
apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1
kind: ApplicationSet
metadata:
  name: api-service
  namespace: argocd
spec:
  generators:
    - list:
        elements:
          - env: dev
            cluster: https://kubernetes.default.svc
            revision: main
          - env: staging
            cluster: https://staging.example.com
            revision: main
          - env: prod
            cluster: https://prod.example.com
            revision: release-1.4
  template:
    metadata:
      name: 'api-service-{{env}}'
    spec:
      project: '{{env}}'
      source:
        repoURL: https://github.com/org/k8s-config
        targetRevision: '{{revision}}'
        path: 'apps/api-service/overlays/{{env}}'
      destination:
        server: '{{cluster}}'
        namespace: 'api-{{env}}'
      syncPolicy:
        automated:
          prune: true
          selfHeal: true

items.title: Pin production to a tag or branch, not HEAD
items.description: Tracking main on production means any merge ships to prod. That is fine for dev. It is not fine for prod. Use a release branch or signed tag for prod and let staging track main. Promotion becomes a controlled merge instead of a race.

codeBlocks.label: Different revisions per environment
codeBlocks.code:
# Dev: track main, deploy on every merge
spec:
  source:
    targetRevision: main

# Staging: track a moving release-candidate branch
spec:
  source:
    targetRevision: release-candidate

# Prod: pinned to a release branch, updated by PR
spec:
  source:
    targetRevision: release-1.4

# Or pin to an immutable tag
spec:
  source:
    targetRevision: v1.4.2

codeBlocks.label: Promote by merging branches
codeBlocks.code:
# Promote main to staging
git checkout release-candidate
git merge --ff-only main
git push origin release-candidate

# Promote staging to prod (via PR for review)
git checkout release-1.4
git merge --no-ff release-candidate
# open PR -> require 2 approvals -> merge

# Argo CD picks up each environment on its own schedule

items.title: Define one AppProject per environment
items.description: AppProjects are the security boundary inside Argo CD. Without them, any Application can deploy any resource to any cluster. Create one project per environment so dev apps cannot accidentally target the prod cluster, and staging cannot deploy ClusterRoles.

codeBlocks.label: AppProject for production
codeBlocks.code:
apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1
kind: AppProject
metadata:
  name: prod
  namespace: argocd
spec:
  description: Production workloads

  sourceRepos:
    - https://github.com/org/k8s-config

  destinations:
    - server: https://prod.example.com
      namespace: 'api-prod'
    - server: https://prod.example.com
      namespace: 'web-prod'

  clusterResourceWhitelist:
    - group: ''
      kind: Namespace

  namespaceResourceBlacklist:
    - group: ''
      kind: ResourceQuota
    - group: ''
      kind: LimitRange

  roles:
    - name: deployer
      policies:
        - p, proj:prod:deployer, applications, sync, prod/*, allow
        - p, proj:prod:deployer, applications, get, prod/*, allow
      groups:
        - org:platform-ops

items.title: Isolate environments by cluster or namespace
items.description: Pick one: separate clusters per environment, or shared cluster with strict namespace isolation. Separate clusters cost more but eliminate blast radius. Shared clusters are cheaper but you must enforce NetworkPolicies, ResourceQuotas, and PodSecurity admission. Do not pretend a shared cluster is isolated when you have not configured these.

codeBlocks.label: Namespace with quotas and policies
codeBlocks.code:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: api-prod
  labels:
    environment: production
    pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce: restricted
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: api-prod-quota
  namespace: api-prod
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: '20'
    requests.memory: 40Gi
    limits.cpu: '40'
    limits.memory: 80Gi
    persistentvolumeclaims: '10'
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: deny-cross-namespace
  namespace: api-prod
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes: [Ingress]
  ingress:
    - from:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              environment: production

items.title: Automated sync for dev, gated for prod
items.description: Auto-sync on dev keeps the feedback loop tight. Auto-sync on prod means a typo in main goes live before anyone reviews it. Disable auto-sync on prod and require a manual sync (or a PR-driven release branch update) so a human looks at the diff before traffic moves.

codeBlocks.label: Dev: auto-sync with self-heal
codeBlocks.code:
spec:
  syncPolicy:
    automated:
      prune: true
      selfHeal: true
    syncOptions:
      - CreateNamespace=true
      - ApplyOutOfSyncOnly=true
    retry:
      limit: 3
      backoff:
        duration: 10s
        factor: 2
        maxDuration: 2m

codeBlocks.label: Prod: manual sync only
codeBlocks.code:
spec:
  syncPolicy:
    # No automated block. A human runs:
    #   argocd app sync api-service-prod
    # after reviewing the diff.
    syncOptions:
      - CreateNamespace=false
      - ServerSideApply=true
      - RespectIgnoreDifferences=true
  ignoreDifferences:
    - group: apps
      kind: Deployment
      jsonPointers:
        - /spec/replicas  # HPA owns this

items.title: Keep secrets out of Git, always
items.description: Plain Secrets in your config repo is a breach waiting to happen. Pick one of: External Secrets Operator (pulls from Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, GCP), Sealed Secrets (encrypted in repo, decrypted in cluster), or SOPS (encrypted files Argo CD decrypts via a plugin). Pick one and use it everywhere. Mixing approaches is how secrets leak.

codeBlocks.label: External Secret per environment
codeBlocks.code:
# apps/api-service/overlays/prod/external-secret.yaml
apiVersion: external-secrets.io/v1beta1
kind: ExternalSecret
metadata:
  name: api-db
  namespace: api-prod
spec:
  refreshInterval: 1h
  secretStoreRef:
    name: aws-secrets-manager-prod
    kind: ClusterSecretStore
  target:
    name: api-db
  data:
    - secretKey: DATABASE_URL
      remoteRef:
        key: prod/api-service/db
        property: connection_string

codeBlocks.label: Sealed Secret per environment
codeBlocks.code:
# Each environment uses its own sealing key
kubectl create secret generic api-db \
  --from-literal=password=$(op read 'op://prod/api/db-password') \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml \
  | kubeseal --controller-namespace=kube-system \
             --scope=namespace-wide \
             --namespace=api-prod \
             -o yaml > apps/api-service/overlays/prod/api-db-sealed.yaml

# A sealed secret encrypted for staging will not decrypt in prod
# That is the point. Do not share keys across environments.

items.title: Promote with pull requests, not by editing prod folders directly
items.description: Direct edits to overlays/prod skip review and audit. Wire up a workflow where promotion opens a PR that bumps the image tag, runs validation, and waits for approval. Your audit trail becomes git log and your rollback becomes git revert.

codeBlocks.label: Promotion workflow
codeBlocks.code:
# .github/workflows/promote-to-prod.yaml
name: Promote to prod
on:
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      version:
        description: Image tag from staging
        required: true

jobs:
  promote:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Bump prod overlay
        run: |
          cd apps/api-service/overlays/prod
          kustomize edit set image api-service=api-service:${{ inputs.version }}

      - name: Validate manifests
        run: |
          kustomize build apps/api-service/overlays/prod | \
            kubeconform -strict -summary -

      - uses: peter-evans/create-pull-request@v6
        with:
          branch: promote/api-${{ inputs.version }}
          title: 'Promote api-service ${{ inputs.version }} to prod'
          labels: production,promotion
          reviewers: platform-oncall

items.title: Lock down the config repo with branch protection
items.description: Your config repo is now your deployment control plane. Anyone who can push to main can deploy anywhere. Require PR reviews, signed commits on the prod branch, status checks for manifest validation, and CODEOWNERS for sensitive paths.

codeBlocks.label: CODEOWNERS for sensitive paths
codeBlocks.code:
# .github/CODEOWNERS

# Anything under prod needs platform team approval
/clusters/prod/                    @org/platform-team
/apps/*/overlays/prod/             @org/platform-team

# Cluster bootstrap and AppProjects need security review
/clusters/**/*root-app.yaml        @org/platform-team @org/security
/projects/                         @org/security

# Service teams own their dev and staging overlays
/apps/api-service/overlays/dev/    @org/api-team
/apps/api-service/overlays/staging/ @org/api-team

codeBlocks.label: Required status checks
codeBlocks.code:
# In GitHub branch protection for main:
# - Require pull request reviews: 2 approvals
# - Dismiss stale reviews on new commits
# - Require review from CODEOWNERS
# - Require status checks:
#     kustomize-build
#     kubeconform-validate
#     conftest-policy
#     argocd-diff-preview
# - Require signed commits
# - Include administrators

# Verify with gh CLI:
gh api repos/org/k8s-config/branches/main/protection

items.title: Set sync windows on the prod project
items.description: Even with manual sync, you do not want someone shipping at 4am Friday. Sync windows let you allow deploys during business hours and block them during change freezes. The window applies to auto-sync; you can still run manual sync if you flip the override.

codeBlocks.label: Sync windows on prod project
codeBlocks.code:
apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1
kind: AppProject
metadata:
  name: prod
  namespace: argocd
spec:
  syncWindows:
    - kind: allow
      schedule: '0 9-16 * * 1-4'   # Mon-Thu, 9am-4pm
      duration: 7h
      applications: ['*']
      manualSync: true

    - kind: deny
      schedule: '0 0 * * 5,6,0'    # Fri-Sun all day
      duration: 24h
      applications: ['*']
      manualSync: false

    - kind: deny
      schedule: '0 0 20 12 *'      # Holiday freeze: Dec 20 onwards
      duration: 336h
      applications: ['*']

items.title: Wire up drift alerts and Slack notifications
items.description: When prod drifts from Git, you want to know in seconds, not at the next standup. Argo CD Notifications hooks into sync failures, health degradation, and out-of-sync events. Push them to Slack or PagerDuty so the on-call sees them.

codeBlocks.label: Notification triggers
codeBlocks.code:
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: argocd-notifications-cm
  namespace: argocd
data:
  service.slack: |
    token: $slack-token

  trigger.on-sync-failed: |
    - when: app.status.operationState.phase in ['Error', 'Failed']
      send: [slack-alert]

  trigger.on-health-degraded: |
    - when: app.status.health.status == 'Degraded'
      send: [slack-alert]

  trigger.on-out-of-sync: |
    - when: app.status.sync.status == 'OutOfSync' and app.metadata.labels.environment == 'production'
      send: [slack-alert]

  template.slack-alert: |
    slack:
      attachments: |
        [{
          "title": "{{.app.metadata.name}}",
          "color": "#ff0000",
          "fields": [
            {"title": "Sync", "value": "{{.app.status.sync.status}}"},
            {"title": "Health", "value": "{{.app.status.health.status}}"},
            {"title": "Revision", "value": "{{.app.status.sync.revision}}"}
          ]
        }]

codeBlocks.label: Subscribe an app to a channel
codeBlocks.code:
# Annotate the Application to route alerts
kubectl annotate application api-service-prod \
  -n argocd \
  notifications.argoproj.io/subscribe.on-sync-failed.slack=platform-alerts \
  notifications.argoproj.io/subscribe.on-health-degraded.slack=platform-alerts

# Verify drift in real time
argocd app diff api-service-prod
argocd app get api-service-prod --refresh

items.title: Document the layout in a README at the repo root
items.description: Six months from now a new engineer needs to ship to staging. If they have to reverse-engineer your folder structure from Argo CD's UI, you have already lost. A short README explaining where each environment lives, how to add a service, and how promotion works pays for itself the first time someone uses it.

codeBlocks.label: README.md template
codeBlocks.code:
cat > README.md << 'EOF'
# k8s-config

Argo CD reads from this repo. Pushing to main deploys to dev. Promoting to
staging and prod is gated.

## Layout

    apps/<service>/base/                shared manifests
    apps/<service>/overlays/<env>/      per-environment patches
    clusters/<env>/root-app.yaml        bootstrap Application for the cluster
    clusters/<env>/apps/                child Application manifests
    projects/                           AppProject definitions (RBAC boundary)
    infrastructure/                     ingress, cert-manager, monitoring

## Adding a new service

1. Create apps/<service>/base/ with deployment, service, kustomization.yaml
2. Create overlays/dev, overlays/staging, overlays/prod
3. Add an Application manifest to clusters/dev/apps/<service>.yaml
4. Open a PR. CI runs kustomize build + kubeconform.

## Promoting to prod

Run the "Promote to prod" workflow in GitHub Actions. It opens a PR
bumping the image tag in overlays/prod/. Two approvals required.

## Rollback

    git revert <commit-sha>
    git push origin main

Argo CD will sync the reverted state on the next reconcile.
EOF
